source_text: I really miss my little sister.

reference_translations.text: Sento molto la mancanza della mia sorellina.
reference_translations.notes: Diminutivo sorellina carries affection, not size.

reference_translations.text: Mi manca tantissimo la mia sorellina.
reference_translations.notes: mancare construction; sorellina still the target alterato.

explanation: 'I really miss my little sister' becomes 'Sento molto la mancanza della mia sorellina.' The diminutivo -ina on sorella is affectionate here, not a claim about height; the alterato does the emotional work that English carries with 'little'.

source_text: Wait just a moment, I'm nearly ready.

reference_translations.text: Aspetta un momentino, sono quasi pronto.
Aspetta un attimino, sono quasi pronta.

explanation: 'Wait just a moment' is naturally 'Aspetta un momentino.' The diminutivo -ino on momento makes the wait sound small and friendly; momentino and attimino are the idiomatic softeners here.

source_text: They finally shut the building's main door.

reference_translations.text: Hanno finalmente chiuso il portone del palazzo.
reference_translations.notes: Accrescitivo with gender shift: la porta > il portone.

reference_translations.text: Finalmente hanno chiuso il portone del palazzo.
Hanno finalmente chiuso la portona del palazzo.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: keeps the feminine; -one shifts la porta to masculine il portone.

explanation: 'The building's main door' is il portone: the accrescitivo -one enlarges la porta and flips it masculine, so it is il portone, not la portona. The gender shift is the whole point of the item.

source_text: What awful weather today, it never stops raining!

reference_translations.text: Che tempaccio oggi, non smette mai di piovere!
Che tempaccio oggi, piove in continuazione!

explanation: 'What awful weather' is 'Che tempaccio!' The peggiorativo -accio on tempo marks bad quality, not size, which is exactly the sense of English 'awful' here.

source_text: Ha detto una parolaccia davanti alla maestra.

reference_translations.text: He said a swear word in front of the teacher.
He used a bad word in front of the teacher.

explanation: 'Ha detto una parolaccia' means 'He said a swear word.' Reading the peggiorativo -accia as 'a big word' (parolona) would miss it: -accia marks the word as bad, not large.

source_text: Il pittore ha lasciato la tela sul cavalletto.

reference_translations.text: The painter left the canvas on the easel.
reference_translations.notes: cavalletto is a falso alterato: easel, not a small horse.

reference_translations.text: The painter left the painting on the easel.

explanation: 'Sul cavalletto' means 'on the easel.' Cavalletto is a falso alterato: despite the shape cavallo + -etto, it does not mean 'small horse'. Rendering it 'little horse' is the trap the item guards against.

source_text: A colazione mangiamo spesso la focaccia.

reference_translations.text: For breakfast we often eat focaccia.
reference_translations.notes: focaccia is its own word, a flatbread; not a 'bad seal'.

reference_translations.text: We often have focaccia for breakfast.

explanation: 'La focaccia' is simply focaccia, a flatbread. It looks like foca + -accia but is a falso alterato: the base foca (seal) has nothing to do with the meaning, so you keep the word as focaccia.

source_text: This morning the train was absolutely packed.

reference_translations.text: Stamattina il treno era strapieno.
Il treno era strapieno stamattina.

explanation: 'Absolutely packed' is strapieno: the prefisso intensivo stra- pushes pieno to its limit, working like pienissimo. It attaches straight to the adjective, so no separate 'molto' is needed.

reference_translations.text: I'm utterly fed up of these arguments.
I'm sick and tired of these arguments.

explanation: 'Sono arcistufo' means 'I'm utterly fed up.' The prefisso intensivo arci- raises stufo to a superlative force (like stufissimo); English carries it with 'utterly' or 'sick and tired'.

source_text: There's a party for the new graduates on Friday.

reference_translations.text: Venerdì c'è una festa per i neolaureati.
C'è una festa per i neolaureati venerdì.

explanation: 'The new graduates' is i neolaureati: the prefisso neo- means 'newly', so neo- + laureati = those who have just graduated. It packs English 'new(ly) graduated' into one word.

source_text: Ho incontrato la mia ex moglie al mercato.

reference_translations.text: I met my ex-wife at the market.
reference_translations.notes: ex = former, written as a separate word before the noun.

reference_translations.text: I ran into my ex-wife at the market.

explanation: 'La mia ex moglie' means 'my ex-wife.' Ex means 'former' and stands as a separate word before moglie; it points to the past, the opposite of neo- ('new').
